title: Humphrey Prideaux

infobox.value: Westminster School London  Liskeard Grammar School

(3 May 1648 – 1 November 1724) was a Cornish churchman and orientalist, [Dean of Norwich](/wiki/Dean_of_Norwich "Dean of Norwich") from 1702. His sympathies inclined to [Low Churchism](/wiki/Low_Church "Low Church") in religion and to [Whiggism](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism") in politics.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British clergyman and Dean of Norwich

| Humphrey Prideaux | |
| --- | --- |
| [Dean of Norwich](/wiki/Dean_of_Norwich "Dean of Norwich") | |
|  | |
| Church | [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") |
| Diocese | [Diocese of Norwich](/wiki/Diocese_of_Norwich "Diocese of Norwich") |
| In office | 1702-1714 |
| Personal details | |
| Born | (1648-05-03)3 May 1648 |
| Died | 1 November 1724(1724-11-01) (aged 76) |
| Nationality | British |

**Humphrey Prideaux** (3 May 1648 – 1 November 1724) was a Cornish churchman and orientalist, [Dean of Norwich](/wiki/Dean_of_Norwich "Dean of Norwich") from 1702. His sympathies inclined to [Low Churchism](/wiki/Low_Church "Low Church") in religion and to [Whiggism](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism") in politics.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humphrey_Prideaux&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

The third son of Edmond Prideaux, he was born at [Padstow](/wiki/Padstow "Padstow"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), on 3 May 1648. His mother was a daughter of [John Moyle](/wiki/John_Moyle_(politician) "John Moyle (politician)").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896352-1) After education at [Liskeard grammar school](/wiki/Liskeard_grammar_school "Liskeard grammar school") and [Bodmin grammar school](/w/index.php?title=Bodmin_grammar_school&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bodmin grammar school (page does not exist)"), he went to [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") under [Richard Busby](/wiki/Richard_Busby "Richard Busby"), recommended by his uncle [William Morice](/wiki/William_Morice_(Secretary_of_State) "William Morice (Secretary of State)").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) On 11 December 1668 he matriculated at [Christ Church, Oxford](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Oxford "Christ Church, Oxford"), where he had obtained a studentship.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He graduated B.A. 22 June 1672, M.A. 29 April 1675, B.D. 15 November 1682, D.D. 8 June 1686.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896352-1) In January 1674, Prideaux recorded in his letters a visit to his home of [William Levett](/wiki/William_Levett_(dean) "William Levett (dean)"); with Levett came [Lord Cornbury](/wiki/Henry_Hyde,_2nd_Earl_of_Clarendon "Henry Hyde, 2nd Earl of Clarendon"), son of the [Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"), Levett's principal patron. In other letters, Prideaux mentioned alliances with Levett in ongoing church political manoeuvrings.[[4]](#cite_note-4) At the university he was known for scholarship; [John Fell](/wiki/John_Fell_(clergyman) "John Fell (clergyman)") employed him in 1672 on an edition of *[Florus](/wiki/Florus "Florus")*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896352-1) He also worked on [Edmund Chilmead](/wiki/Edmund_Chilmead "Edmund Chilmead")'s edition of the chronicle of [John Malalas](/wiki/John_Malalas "John Malalas").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2)

Prideaux gained the patronage of [Heneage Finch, 1st Earl of Nottingham](/wiki/Heneage_Finch,_1st_Earl_of_Nottingham "Heneage Finch, 1st Earl of Nottingham"), as tutor to his son Charles,[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) and in 1677 he obtained the sinecure rectory of [Llandewy-Velfrey](/w/index.php?title=Llandewy-Velfrey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Llandewy-Velfrey (page does not exist)"), [Pembrokeshire](/wiki/Pembrokeshire "Pembrokeshire"). In 1679 Finch presented him to the rectory of [St Clement's, Oxford](/wiki/St_Clement%27s,_Oxford "St Clement's, Oxford"), which he held till 1696. He was appointed also, in 1679, Busby's Hebrew lecturer in Christ Church College. Finch gave him in 1681 a canonry at [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), and [Sir Francis North](/wiki/Sir_Francis_North "Sir Francis North") in February 1683 presented him to the rectory of [Bladon](/wiki/Bladon "Bladon"), [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire"), which included the chapelry of [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock,_Oxfordshire "Woodstock, Oxfordshire"). He retained his studentship at Christ Church, where he was acting as unsalaried librarian.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896352-1)

Prideaux married and left Oxford for Norwich, ahead of James II's appointment (October 1686) of [John Massey](/w/index.php?title=John_Massey_(Dean_of_Christ_Church)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Massey (Dean of Christ Church) (page does not exist)"), a Roman Catholic, as Dean of Christ Church. He exchanged (1686) Bladon for the rectory of [Saham-Toney](/w/index.php?title=Saham-Toney&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saham-Toney (page does not exist)"), Norfolk, which he held till 1694. He engaged in controversy with Roman Catholics, especially on the point of the validity of [Anglican orders](/wiki/Anglican_orders "Anglican orders"). As canon of Norwich he improved the financial arrangements of the chapter, and put the records in order. In December 1688 he was made [archdeacon of Suffolk](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Suffolk "Archdeacon of Suffolk") by his bishop [William Lloyd](/wiki/William_Lloyd_(bishop_of_Norwich) "William Lloyd (bishop of Norwich)"), an office which he held till 1694. While Lloyd became a [nonjuror](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism"), Prideaux exerted himself at his archidiaconal visitation (May 1689) to secure the taking of the oaths; out of three hundred parishes in his archdeaconry only three clergymen became nonjurors. At the [Convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") which opened on 21 November 1689 Prideaux was an advocate for changes in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*, with a view to the comprehension of [Dissenters](/wiki/Dissenters "Dissenters"). Subsequently he officially corrected a lax interpretation of the [Toleration Act 1688](/wiki/Toleration_Act_1688 "Toleration Act 1688"), as though it exempted from the duty of attendance on public worship. [Gilbert Burnet](/wiki/Gilbert_Burnet "Gilbert Burnet") consulted him in 1691 about a measure for prevention of [pluralities](/wiki/Pluralities "Pluralities"), and Prideaux drafted a bill for this purpose. [Richard Kidder](/wiki/Richard_Kidder "Richard Kidder") consulted him in the same year about a bill for preventing [clandestine marriages](/wiki/Clandestine_marriage "Clandestine marriage").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896352–353-5)

From 1689 to 1694 Prideaux resided at Saham. He declined in 1691 the Oxford Hebrew chair vacated by the death of [Edward Pococke](/wiki/Edward_Pococke "Edward Pococke"), a step which he afterwards regretted. Saham did not suit his health, and he returned to Norwich. In a letter written on 28 November 1694 after receiving the news of [John Tillotson](/wiki/John_Tillotson "John Tillotson")'s death, he said that he had no expectations of future advancement. Early in 1697 he was presented to the vicarage of [Trowse](/wiki/Trowse "Trowse"), near Norwich, a chapter living, which he held till 1709. He succeeded [Henry Fairfax](/wiki/Henry_Fairfax_(dean) "Henry Fairfax (dean)") as dean of Norwich, and was installed on 8 June 1702. On the translation to Ely (31 July 1707) of [John Moore](/wiki/John_Moore_(bishop_of_Ely) "John Moore (bishop of Ely)"), Prideaux recommended the appointment of [Charles Trimnell](/wiki/Charles_Trimnell "Charles Trimnell"), his fellow canon, as bishop.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6)

In 1721 Prideaux gave his collection of oriental books to [Clare Hall, Cambridge](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge"). From about 1709 he had suffered severely from [the stone](/wiki/Bladder_stone "Bladder stone"), which prevented him from preaching. An operation was badly managed; attacks of rheumatism and paralysis reduced his strength. He died on 1 November 1724, at the deanery, Norwich, and was buried in the nave of the [Norwich Cathedral](/wiki/Norwich_Cathedral "Norwich Cathedral"), where there was a stone to his memory, with an epitaph composed by himself.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6)

## Works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humphrey_Prideaux&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Works")]

Among his other works were a *Life of Mahomet* (1697),[[7]](#cite_note-7) with a polemical tract against the [deists](/wiki/Deist "Deist").[[8]](#cite_note-eb1911-8) Its scholarship depended in particular on Pococke.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) It was criticised by [George Sale](/wiki/George_Sale "George Sale"), in his notes to his translation of the *[Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran")*.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6) Its year-of-publication reprint was followed by a third, and a fourth; and four further editions between 1712 and 1718, and yet another reprint in 1723.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/OldandNewTestamentPrideaux1718.jpg/250px-OldandNewTestamentPrideaux1718.jpg)](/wiki/File:OldandNewTestamentPrideaux1718.jpg)

*The Old and New Testament connected*, title page to fifth edition of 1718.

*The Old and New Testament connected in the History of the Jews and Neighbouring Nations* (1715–17)[[10]](#cite_note-10) was a significant work, of which many editions were brought out; it drew on [James Ussher](/wiki/James_Ussher "James Ussher").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) It was among the earliest English works to use the term *[Vulgar Era](/wiki/Vulgar_Era "Vulgar Era")*,[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) though Kepler used the term as early as 1635.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It covered the historical interval between the Old and New Testaments, and led to a controversy between Prideaux and his cousin, [Walter Moyle](/wiki/Walter_Moyle "Walter Moyle"). [Jean Le Clerc](/wiki/Jean_Leclerc_(theologian) "Jean Leclerc (theologian)") wrote a critical examination of it, which was published in English in 1722.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896354-14) The French translation was by [Moses Solanus](/wiki/Moses_Solanus "Moses Solanus") and [Jean-Baptiste Brutel de la Rivière](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Brutel_de_la_Rivi%C3%A8re "Jean-Baptiste Brutel de la Rivière").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

He published the following pamphlets: *The Validity of the Orders of the Church of England* (1688), *Letter to a Friend on the Present Convocation* (1690), *The Case of Clandestine Marriages stated* (1691).[[8]](#cite_note-eb1911-8) Other works were:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Die_schwebenden_G%C3%A4rten_von_Babylon_1726.jpg/250px-Die_schwebenden_G%C3%A4rten_von_Babylon_1726.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_schwebenden_G%C3%A4rten_von_Babylon_1726.jpg)

The [Hanging Gardens of Babylon](/wiki/Hanging_Gardens_of_Babylon "Hanging Gardens of Babylon"), engraving from the German edition of 1726 of *The Old and New Testament connected*.

* *Marmora Oxoniensia*, &c., Oxford, 1676, a catalogue and chronology of the [Arundel marbles](/wiki/Arundel_marbles "Arundel marbles") and related collections; the numerous typographical errors were ascribed to the carelessness of [Thomas Bennet](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Bennet_(grammarian)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thomas Bennet (grammarian) (page does not exist)") as corrector of the press.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6) There were later editions, by [Michael Maittaire](/wiki/Michael_Maittaire "Michael Maittaire") and [Richard Chandler](/wiki/Richard_Chandler_(antiquary) "Richard Chandler (antiquary)").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* *De Jure Pauperis et Peregrini*, &c., Oxford, 1679, (edition of the Hebrew of [Maimonides](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides"), with a Latin version and notes).
* *A Compendious Introduction for Reading ... Histories*, &c., Oxford, 1682.
* *Directions to Churchwardens*, &c., Norwich, 1701; 7th edition, 1730.
* *The Original and Right of Tithes*, &c., Norwich, 1710; reprinted 1713; 1736.
* *Ecclesiastical Tracts*, &c., 1716.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6)

His letters (1674–1722) to [John Ellis](/wiki/John_Ellis_(Harwich_MP) "John Ellis (Harwich MP)") were edited for the [Camden Society](/wiki/Camden_Society "Camden Society") in 1875 by [Edward Maunde Thompson](/wiki/Edward_Maunde_Thompson "Edward Maunde Thompson").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896354-14)

## Marriage and children

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humphrey_Prideaux&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Marriage and children")]

On 16 February 1686 Prideaux married Bridget Bokenham, only child of Anthony Bokenham of [Helmingham](/wiki/Helmingham "Helmingham"), Suffolk, and left a son:

* [Edmund Prideaux](/wiki/Edmund_Prideaux_(1693-1745) "Edmund Prideaux (1693-1745)") (1693-1745) of [Prideaux Place](/wiki/Prideaux_Place "Prideaux Place"), [Padstow](/wiki/Padstow "Padstow"), Cornwall, a lawyer of the [Middle Temple](/wiki/Middle_Temple "Middle Temple")[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1896353-6)[[17]](#cite_note-17) and a talented amateur architectural artist.
